pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Securing Nigeria's food supply - Video

pageCaption.value: In 2010, Nnaemeka Ikegwuonu, a Nigerian social entrepreneur, won a Rolex Award for Enterprise for creating a radio station to advise smallholder farmers.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Securing Nigeria's food supply - Video

metadescription.value: Rolex Awards Laureate Nnaemeka Ikegwuonu is developing projects to strengthen Nigeria's food systems and eliminate wastage. Watch the video on rolex.org.

text.value: In 2010, Nnaemeka Ikegwuonu, a Nigerian social entrepreneur, won a Rolex Award for Enterprise for creating a radio station to advise smallholder farmers. Today, around 40 per cent of Nigeria’s produce rots in the heat before it can be sold. In a country ranked 107th out of 113 countries on the Global Food Security Index – and where more than 88 million people face food insecurity while 12 per cent are undernourished – this wastage is a huge problem. To combat this, Ikegwuonu has created ColdHubs, a network of solar-powered, walk-in cold rooms that is revolutionizing Nigeria’s food supply chain.

pageCaption.value: Tapping into the latest trends in information and telecommunications technology, Jacob Colker has combined volunteering, the internet and mobile phones to pioneer a new form of activism in which almost anyone with a smartphone can devote spare minutes — waiting for the bus or to see the doctor — to a useful charitable or scientific task.

metadescription.value: Tapping into the latest trends in information and telecommunications technology, Jacob Colker has combined volunteering, the internet and mobile phones to pioneer a new form of activism in which almost anyone with a smartphone can devote spare minutes — waiting for the bus or to see the doctor — to a useful charitable or scientific task.

pageTitle.value: Connecting the world with signs - Video

pageCaption.value: The world’s 70 million hearing-impaired people use 126 different languages, each with its own grammar and vocabulary. However, there are very few dictionaries to bridge the gaps between these languages. A Japanese entrepreneur, Junto Ohki, has created SLinto, an online sign-language dictionary that crowdsources signs –which are input with a special keyboard – to help deaf communities across the world converse.

pageTitle.value: Electronic ears to listen to the rainforests - Video

pageCaption.value: Technologist Topher White is giving scientists and conservationists a unique view of wildlife in the world’s rainforests – through an alert system he originally developed to detect illegal logging using old mobile phones.

pageTitle.value: 2021 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate Video: Felix Brooks-church

pageCaption.value: Poor nutrition contributes to 15,000 preventable child deaths daily worldwide. American social entrepreneur Felix Brooks-church has an answer: an ingenious system to ensure that each meal consumed by every mother and infant living in an underprivileged society contains essential, life-saving nutrients. His project is bringing new life and hope to children in Tanzania, as a model for the world.

metadescription.value: Felix Brooks-church is fighting malnutrition in Tanzania by fortifying meals consumed by mothers and infants. Watch the video on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Eradicate malnutrition in Tanzania, one fortified bag at a time - Video

pageTitle.value: Software that cuts food waste - Video

pageCaption.value: Oscar Ekponimo, of Nigeria, is solving an old problem –  malnutrition caused by poverty – with a 21st century invention. His cloud-based app, Chowberry, alerts retailers when products reach the end of shelf life. Discounted food can then be offered to relief agencies, cutting waste. Ekponimo, who suffered from hunger as a child, developed his app to help the millions of Nigerians who do not get enough to eat.

pageTitle.value: Cool food in the desert - Video

pageCaption.value: Nigerian teacher Mohammed Bah Abba was motivated by his concern for the rural poor and by his interest in indigenous African technology to seek a practical, local solution to these problems. His extremely simple and inexpensive earthenware "pot-in-pot" cooling device, based on a principle of physics already known in ancient Egypt, has revolutionized lives in this semi-desert area.
